preFix.title: Making a small corner shelf with mitre joints
preFix.subtitle: Cut and assemble a triangular corner shelf with mitred front edges
preFix.excerpt: How to make a small corner shelf from solid timber or MDF, with 45-degree mitre joints at the front corners for a clean finished edge, and a triangular back edge that sits against the two walls. Covers marking out, cutting the mitre, assembling with glue and pins, and fitting to the wall.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. City and Guilds Level 2 Site Carpentry.

content.text: A small corner shelf, for a bathroom, a hallway, or a bedroom corner, is a good first joinery project. It uses one

at the front corner and two simple straight cuts at the back where the shelf meets the walls. The back corners are hidden so they do not need to be precise; the front mitre is the visible joint and deserves care.

One piece of 18 mm solid timber or MDF, approximately 300 × 300 mm (sufficient for one corner shelf of 200 mm depth). PVA wood glue. Finish nails or a small panel pin. Two angle brackets or timber keyhole-plate brackets for wall fixing. Appropriate wall fixings (plugs and screws for masonry, or long screws into studs for plasterboard walls).
Step 1, mark out the shelf shape

Mark the shelf on the board. A simple triangular corner shelf has two equal back edges (each equal to the intended shelf depth, typically 180 to 220 mm for a bathroom shelf) and a single mitred front edge. Mark the back corner at 90 degrees and the two back edges at equal length from that corner. The front edge runs from the end of one back edge to the end of the other, crossing the corner diagonally. Mark the mitre cut on each front piece: each front board piece is cut at 45 degrees where it meets the other piece at the centre front corner.
Step 2, cut the back edges and the mitre

Cut the two back edges square with the tenon saw. Cut the 45-degree mitre on each front piece by setting the combination square to 45 degrees and marking the line carefully on both pieces. Cut on the waste side of the line with the tenon saw. Test the dry fit: the two mitred faces should close cleanly at the front corner with no gap and both back edges should sit flat against the walls. Minor adjustments can be made by sanding the mitre face at a low angle against the sanding block.

Apply PVA glue to both mitre faces and press together. Drive two panel pins through the corner at an angle to pull the joint tight while the glue sets. Wipe away any squeezed-out glue with a damp cloth. Allow the joint to cure fully (at least 2 hours) before sanding or fixing.
Step 4, sand, finish, and fix to the wall

Sand all faces with the sanding block, starting at 100 grit and finishing at 150 grit. Finish as desired, paint, varnish, or oil. Fix two angle brackets on the underside of the shelf at the back corners, positioning them so the screws will go into the wall at the correct height. Hold the shelf at the intended position, check it is level with the spirit level, mark the fixing positions, and fix.

attrs.fix: If caught before assembly: plane or sand the mitre face to close the gap, testing regularly. If the glue has cured: fill the gap with wood filler in a matching colour, sand flush, and paint.
attrs.cause: One or both mitre cuts were not at exactly 45 degrees.
attrs.symptom: A small gap appears at the front mitre joint

attrs.fix: Mark the actual wall angle by holding both shelf back edges against the walls simultaneously and scribing the gap. Adjust the back edge cuts slightly to match the actual wall angle. The front mitre still closes at a true 45 degrees because the front edge runs perpendicular to the corner bisector regardless of the room angle.
attrs.cause: The internal corner is not at exactly 90 degrees; most room corners are a few degrees out.
attrs.symptom: The shelf does not sit flat against both walls

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
